title: Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) — Bill C-27
official_title: Part 3 of Bill C-27: Digital Charter Implementation Act, 2022 — Artificial Intelligence and Data Act
status: Proposed — Bill C-27 passed second reading; AIDA subject to parliamentary study
overview: AIDA is Canada's proposed federal AI law, forming Part 3 of Bill C-27 which also includes the Consumer Privacy Protection Act (CPPA) to replace PIPEDA. AIDA focuses on 'high-impact AI systems' — those that have a significant effect on the lives of individuals or that present a risk of significant harm. It creates obligations for persons who design, develop, make available, or manage high-impact AI systems. Note: Bill C-27 died on the Order Paper when Parliament prorogued in January 2025; the legislation will need to be reintroduced by the new government. Monitoring for reintroduction is recommended.

legislative_status.parliament_status: Died on Order Paper — Parliament prorogued January 2025
legislative_status.reintroduction_expected: To be determined by incoming government
legislative_status.note: Despite lapse, AIDA reflects Canadian government policy direction. Organisations should monitor closely and prepare as if AIDA will be enacted in substantially similar form.

key_definitions.definition: An AI system that meets criteria established by regulation — likely covering AI used in employment decisions, credit/insurance decisions, criminal justice, health decisions, and critical infrastructure. Final regulatory criteria not yet published.
key_definitions.significance: The definition is not yet finalized; the government will define high-impact through subordinate regulation, creating uncertainty.

key_definitions.description: A person who, in the course of international or interprovincial trade and commerce, (a) designs, develops, or makes available for use a high-impact AI system; or (b) manages the operations of a high-impact AI system.

proposed_obligations.title: Risk assessment
proposed_obligations.description: Regulated persons must assess whether their AI system is high-impact and document that assessment.

proposed_obligations.title: Risk mitigation measures
proposed_obligations.description: Regulated persons must establish and implement measures to identify, assess, and mitigate risks of harm or biased output.
proposed_obligations.applies_to: Persons responsible for high-impact AI systems

proposed_obligations.title: Ongoing monitoring
proposed_obligations.description: Regulated persons must monitor high-impact AI systems and collect records of monitoring activities.
proposed_obligations.applies_to: Persons managing high-impact AI systems in operation

proposed_obligations.title: Transparency publication
proposed_obligations.description: Persons making high-impact AI systems available must publish a plain-language description of the system and how it mitigates risks.
proposed_obligations.applies_to: Persons making high-impact AI systems available for use

proposed_obligations.title: Incident notification
proposed_obligations.description: Regulated persons must notify the Minister of Innovation if the system causes or risks causing serious harm or produces outputs that are biased in a way that causes serious harm.

proposed_obligations.title: Record keeping
proposed_obligations.description: Regulated persons must establish and maintain records of their AI system's design, development, operation, and risk management activities.

prohibited_conduct: Making available a high-impact AI system that is likely to cause serious harm without implementing appropriate mitigation measures
Deliberately providing false or misleading information to the Minister or an auditor
Knowingly or recklessly processing data obtained by an unlawful act to train an AI system intended to cause harm

existing_ai_relevant_law.law: PIPEDA (Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act)
existing_ai_relevant_law.relevance: Currently governs collection and use of personal data for AI training and deployment. Automated decision-making disclosure obligations apply. Being replaced by CPPA (Bill C-27 Part 1) if enacted.
AI systems used in employment, services, or accommodation that produce discriminatory effects on prohibited grounds violate the CHRA.
Deceptive AI claims or AI used to facilitate deceptive marketing practices violate the Competition Act.

cross_references.relevance: Canada-EU companies face dual compliance; AIDA and EU AI Act have different high-risk criteria and obligation structures, creating potential double obligations
All AI systems processing personal information about Canadians remain subject to federal privacy law regardless of AIDA status
